items.question: Who uses mutual information in machine learning?
items.answer: ML engineers and data scientists use I(X; Y) for filter-based feature selection, redundancy checks between features, clustering evaluation (NMI), and information-bottleneck-style representation learning; information theorists use it for channel capacity and dependence analysis.

items.question: What is mutual information I(X; Y)?
items.answer: I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X | Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y): the average number of bits (with log base 2) by which knowing Y reduces uncertainty about X. It equals the expectation of log(p(x,y)/(p(x)p(y))) over the joint distribution.

items.question: When is I(X; Y) equal to zero?
items.answer: If and only if X and Y are statistically independent: p(x,y) = p(x)p(y) for all x,y. Then H(X, Y) = H(X) + H(Y) and no information is shared.

items.question: Where does mutual information appear in a feature-selection pipeline?
items.answer: In the filter stage before model training: compute I(X_i; Y) for each candidate feature, rank by score, optionally drop features with low MI or high redundancy I(X_i; X_j), then pass the reduced set to the trainer.

items.question: Why prefer mutual information over Pearson correlation for screening features?
items.answer: Correlation detects only linear association and requires meaningful linear scale. MI detects any dependence in the joint PMF—U-shapes, thresholds, and categorical interactions—making it safer for heterogeneous tabular data before choosing a model family.

items.question: How do you compute I(X; Y) from a small discrete joint table?
items.answer: Estimate p(x,y) from counts, derive marginals p(x) and p(y), then sum p(x,y) log2(p(x,y)/(p(x)p(y))) over pairs with p(x,y) > 0. Verify with H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y) using the same log base.

items.question: Mutual information I(X; Y) vs Pearson correlation r — what does each detect?
items.answer: Pearson r measures linear association scaled to [-1, 1] and can be zero when strong non-linear dependence exists. I(X; Y) is non-negative, measured in bits, and is zero only under independence—so MI catches non-linear and categorical dependence that r misses, while r is simpler and cheaper for strictly linear Gaussian settings.

items.question: I(X; Y) vs KL divergence D_KL(P || Q) — how are they related?
items.answer: KL divergence compares two distributions on the same outcome space and is generally asymmetric. Mutual information is the special case D_KL(P_{X,Y} || P_X P_Y): KL from the true joint to the independence product. MI is symmetric in X and Y; general KL is not.

items.question: What causes I(X; Y) to reach its maximum min(H(X), H(Y))?
items.answer: Cause: Y is a deterministic function of X (or vice versa) on the support—each X value maps to one Y value with probability 1. Effect: H(X | Y) = 0 (or H(Y | X) = 0), so I(X; Y) = H(X) (or H(Y)), the upper bound.

items.question: What causes spuriously high estimated MI on small datasets?
items.answer: Cause: finite-sample noise in estimated p(x,y) from too few observations relative to the number of joint cells. Effect: plug-in MI estimators bias upward, ranking random features as informative until sample size grows or smoothing/permutation tests filter noise.
